title: Raising Capital from Investor Syndicates with Strategic Communication
abstract: An entrepreneur makes offers to multiple investors to fund a project that requires a minimum investment. Concerned about other investors' decisions, each investor strategically communicates information about the project to others. When investors have conflicts of interest, those with contractually stronger incentives to invest attempt to persuade others to invest. Depending on the project's ex ante quality, the entrepreneur may promise investors different returns to create conflicts of interest and induce persuasion, or may promise investors an identical return to align their interests and induce truthful communication. The paper illustrates a new motivation for syndication and hierarchy within syndicates.

article.introduction:
An investor syndicate is atemporary alliance of investors who join together to manage an investment. In practice, projects and firms are commonly financed in this way. Two well-known examples are syndicated loans and venture capital (VC) syndicates. Despite the ubiquity of investor syndicates, most academic research studies the case of a single investor or a competitive market. Such abstraction may ignore a number of important mechanisms. On the one hand, unlike the case of multiple competing investors, investors within a syndicate cooperate in many aspects. For example, they actively share information and divide up labor. As the founder of YCombinator, Paul Graham, wrote in his blog, “By far the biggest influence on investors' opinions of a startup is the opinion of other investors. There are very, very few who simply decide for themselves.”1On the other hand, an investor syndicate does not act like a single investor. Due to various heterogeneities, investors may have divergent interests or conflicts of interest (Alchian and Demsetz (1972), Holmstrom (1982), Nanda and Rhodes-Kropf (2018)).
This paper develops a theory for the formation and structures of investor syndicates and explains various syndication practices in a unified framework. The paper focuses on several important questions: How do investors in syndicates interact with each other and make decisions? Why are syndicates used, and when is it advantageous to use a hierarchical (or asymmetric) versus a flat (or symmetric) syndicate structure? To address these questions, I build a simple model of joint investment. In the model, an entrepreneur owns a project of uncertain type: The project generates positive cash flows if its type is good and zero if it is bad. The project requires a minimum level of investment to be implemented. To raise funds to finance the project, the entrepreneur makes offers to one or multiple investors, with terms that need not be identical. Investors first observe a private signal that may reveal the project's type, then communicate strategically via cheap talk, and finally accept or reject offers.
The model's key mechanism is investors' strategic communication. Investors' payoffs depend on the project outcome, so they would like to obtain information about the project from others to improve investment decisions. However, investors may not truthfully communicate unverifiable information to others if they have conflicts of interest. Although investors are ex ante identical in the model, they may be promised different returns. Consequently, when the project's type is unknown, they may prefer different investment outcomes.2It is possible that when all investors communicate truthfully, investors with high promised returns prefer to invest but others do not. Since project implementation requires a minimum level of investment, an investor who prefers to invest may hope some others can also invest. This complementarity leads to conflicts of interest and may impede truthful communication. Therefore, a novel role of contracts is to determine whether investors have aligned or divergent interests and in turn to affect how these investors strategically communicate.
Characterizing the entrepreneur's optimal contract, the paper obtains three new findings. First, depending on the project's ex ante quality, the optimal contract takes two possible forms. It could beflat, in the sense that it promises identical returns to investors, orhierarchicalin the sense that it promises different returns. While a flat contract can involve only one investor, a hierarchical contract naturally entails a syndicate with at least two investors. Second, hierarchical contracts can be optimal because they impede truthful communication between investors and compromise investors' ability to earn information rents. This motive to use hierarchical contracts hinges on the strategic nature of communication and is novel in the literature. Third, the optimality of hierarchical contracts implies a novel motivation for syndication. The reason that the entrepreneur prefers a hierarchical contract to a single investor is not that such a contract brings in more investors that provide more capital or information, but other than that it allows for persuasion between investors.
I start with the baseline setup in which (i) there are two investors, (ii) they have exogenous information, and (iii) they are subject to capital constraints, so the entrepreneur must raise funds from both investors. I first characterize the investment outcome under any contract. If both investors prefer the same acceptance decision when the project's type is unknown, they do not face the aforementioned conflicts of interest and will achieve the investment outcome that is best for both of them. Otherwise, the investor with the higher promised return will attempt to persuade the other to accept by sending out favorable messages whenever he would like to accept. However, his observation of a bad project is always revealed, because in this case he will not accept and hence the project will not be implemented. Thus, this investor does not credibly communicate good news to others, but still does screen out some bad projects. This screening improves the other's belief about the project's type conditional on project implementation. Under the influence of such persuasion, the other investor may also want to accept when the project's type is unknown, despite his lower promised return. I find that with the refinement that Pareto-dominated equilibria are not played, the investment outcome is always unique under any contract.
Based on the unique equilibrium investment outcome, I next study the entrepreneur's contracting problem. Given that the entrepreneur must raise funds from a syndicate of two investors, I focus on optimal syndicate structures, which I find take two possible forms. When the project has low ex ante quality,3the surplus of implementing a project with an unknown type is low. Hence, the entrepreneur would like to promise investors low returns despite the fact that they accept only when the project's type is known to be good. Investors have high investment thresholds and do not earn information rents. Given the high investment thresholds, the entrepreneur hopes that an investor's observation of a good project is credibly revealed and convinces the other investor to accept. To facilitate truthful communication, the entrepreneur uses a flat contract that promises identical returns to investors and aligns their interests. Under this contract, investors' relevant information is fully revealed. When the project has high ex ante quality, the surplus of implementing a project with an unknown type is high. Hence, the entrepreneur would like to promise investors sufficiently high returns such that they accept even when the project's type is unknown. Investors have low investment thresholds and may earn information rents. Truthful communication between them improves their ability to do so. To impede truthful communication, the entrepreneur uses a hierarchical contract that promises investors different returns and creates conflicts of interest. Under this contract, the investor with the higher promised return attempts to persuade the other investor to accept, so his observation is only partially revealed.
The prediction of the two different structures is consistent with the patterns of investor syndicates observed in practice.4The vast majority of venture capital (VC) syndicates are organized inflat structuresin which investors in the same round almost always invest at the same firm valuation and receive no fees. In my model, this outcome is consistent with a setting in which coordinated communication and screening are needed because good projects are relatively rare. In contrast, most loan syndicates are organized inhierarchical structuresin which lead investors are formally appointed as underwriters or lead arrangers and earn higher returns than other lenders through fee income. This is the outcome predicted in my model when bad projects are relatively rare and the main concern is limiting investors' information rents. Notably, as predicted by the model, hierarchical structures result in communication problems between lead investors and others, which is supported by many legal cases.5
Next, I examine the entrepreneur's optimal syndication decision by extending the baseline setup to include deep-pocketed investors and costly information acquisition. With investors being deep-pocketed, the entrepreneur can decide to raise funds from a single investor or a syndicate of two investors. Costly information acquisition helps determine each investor's deal size and information structure. Similar to the baseline setup, the optimal contract makes the two investors either both willing to invest in an uncertain project or both unwilling, depending on the project's ex ante quality. Among the contracts making investors unwilling to invest in an uncertain project, a single-investor contract or a syndicate with a flat structure is optimal, and the motivation for syndication is the classical “second opinion” (Brander, Amit, and Antweiler (2002)). Syndication brings in more investors acquiring information and therefore leads to a higher probability that any investor observes the project's type. Among the contracts making investors willing to invest in an uncertain project, a syndicate with a hierarchical structure is optimal, and the motivation for syndication is to allow for persuasion between investors. From the entrepreneur's perspective, investors' information helps avoid investment in bad projects, which makes them more eager to invest and increases their ability to earn information rents. Persuasion between investors can reinforce the bright side more than the dark side. Therefore, a syndicate with persuasion between investors is more desirable than a single investor. This motivation is novel in the literature and justifies the existence of pure capital providers without resorting to capital constraints or risk aversion.
In another extension to the baseline setup, I consider a scenario in which project implementation requires investment from more than two investors. I find that investors' persuasion motive gives rise to persuasion cascades in equilibrium: An investor who prefers to invest in an uncertain project due to some other investors' persuasion will attempt to persuade yet more investors to invest in an uncertain project.6Taking advantage of persuasion cascades leads to finer differentiation in investors' promised returns when the optimal contract is hierarchical. Finally, I derive testable model implications that help distinguish my theory from other potential theories.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIreviews the related literature. SectionIIintroduces the baseline setup and presents a simple illustration of the core idea. SectionIIIformally derives the entrepreneur's optimal syndicate structures in the baseline setup. SectionIVstudies the entrepreneur's optimal syndication decisions. SectionVextends the baseline setup to multiple investors and illustrates persuasion cascades. SectionVIdiscusses the model's relation to empirical observations and derives testable implications. SectionVIIconcludes the paper with other applications. All proofs are given in AppendixA.
article.I. Related Literature:
This paper is connected to several strands of theoretical literature. Considering an entrepreneur contracting with multiple investors, my paper belongs to the literature on contracting with externalities initiated by Segal (1999). Most papers in this literature study the implications of strategic risk faced by agents for contracting and model strategic risk by focusing on the worst equilibrium of a coordination game (Segal (2003), Winter (2004), Bernstein and Winter (2012), Akerlof and Holden (2019), Halac, Kremer, and Winter (2020)). This equilibrium selection prohibits investors from providing mutual assurance. Specifically, in the two-investor case, if each investor relies on the other's investment to justify his investment, no one would invest in equilibrium. Since assurance between any two investors must be unidirectional, the entrepreneur's optimal contract promises investors differential returns.7My paper considers a different mechanism through which investors interact: information sharing through strategic communication. Because of communication, investors are able to coordinate to their desired equilibrium when their interests are aligned. Although such coordination eliminates strategic risk, it also prevents investors from persuading each other, which implies that persuasion between any two investors must be unidirectional. Following a similar logic, the entrepreneur's optimal contract also features differential returns when she intends to induce persuasion among investors. Notably, although the optimality of differential returns in the literature and that in my paper both stem from the similar mathematical structure of unidirectional externality, their economic origins are quite different, and even opposite: While the literature relies on coordination failure, my paper relies on coordination among investors when their interests are aligned. In addition, in my paper, identical returns may also arise in the optimal contract if the entrepreneur chooses not to induce persuasion among investors.
Studying how to use contracts to affect agents' communication, my paper is related to the literature on financial advice (Bolton, Freixas, and Shapiro (2007), Inderst and Ottaviani (2009,2012,2013), Heider and Inderst (2012)). This literature studies how sellers design commissions to control financial advisors' communication with consumers. My paper differs from this literature by considering a multiagent communication game in which an agent can potentially both send and receive information.
My paper is also related to the extensive literature on information transmission (Crawford and Sobel (1982), Rayo and Segal (2010), Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011)). Two strands in this literature are particularly relevant. The first strand is multiagent communication. Hagenbach and Koessler (2010) and Galeotti, Ghiglino, and Squintani (2013) find that communication between two players depends on not only conflicts of interest between them but also on the number and preferences of the other players with whom they communicate. In my paper, such dependence on other players is reflected by persuasion cascades. The second strand is informational cascades. Typically, informational cascades occur through agents' observational learning (Bikhchandani, Hirshleifer, and Welch (1992), Callander (2007), Guarino, Harmgart, and Huck (2011), Herrera and Hörner (2013), Cong and Xiao (2024)) or planners' information design (Caillaud and Tirole (2007), Kremer, Mansour, and Perry (2014)). The key mechanism is that agents can observe others' actions but cannot convey or obtain finer information. My paper contributes to this literature in two respects. First, it uncovers and characterizes a novel form of informational cascades in the presence of agents' strategic communication—persuasion cascades. The novelty of the persuasion cascades here is that they stem from agents' desire to persuade others rather than their inability to convey finer information. Second, my paper studies how the principal's contract design can exploit persuasion cascades to affect agents' decisions, which distinguishes my paper from those studying information design (Caillaud and Tirole (2007), Kremer, Mansour, and Perry (2014)). Unlike the typical informational cascades, persuasion cascades based on strategic communication imply that optimal contracts may intentionally create conflicts of interest by offering different contract terms to agents.8
Finally, my paper is related to the large literature on committee decision making. Most papers in this literature take the relevant payoff of committee members as given and focus on how decision rules or committee characteristics affect welfare. Coughlan (2000), Austen-Smith and Feddersen (2006), Harris and Raviv (2008), and Malenko (2014) consider communication or deliberation before voting. Depending on the setting, communication is modeled as cheap talk or costly verifiable reports. In particular, Li, Rosen, and Suen (2001) point out that if conflicts of interest are present, committee members would like to manipulate the information they communicate to others and thus garbling occurs. Khanna and Schroder (2015) study the payoff structure of committee members that incentivizes their information acquisition and truthful signal reporting. They find that the optimal payoff structure rewards a committee member for being correct while also disagreeing with others.
Turning to the application to investor syndicates, my paper is related to the literature on syndicated finance. This literature puts forward several motivations for syndication. The “diversification” hypothesis argues that syndication is a means of risk-sharing (Lockett and Wright (1999)). The “second opinion” hypothesis and the “value-added” hypothesis argue that syndication is an effective way to gather useful information for deal screening and value-added inputs from multiple investors (Lerner (1994), Brander, Amit, and Antweiler (2002), Casamatta and Haritchabalet (2007), Cestone, Lerner, and White (2007), Bayar, Chemmanur, and Tian (2020)). These papers are silent, however, on syndicate structures. My paper proposes a novel motivation for syndication—allowing for persuasion between investors—and explains various syndicate structures in a unified framework that, to my knowledge, is the first in the literature.
article.II. The Baseline Setup and a Simple Illu:
This section introduces the baseline setup and illustrates the paper's core idea in an informal way.
A penniless entrepreneur (“she”) needs an amount of capital, which is normalized to one, to implement a project. There are two investors (“he”), each of which has one half of the capital needed. All players are risk-neutral, and their outside options are zero. The project is either good or bad. A bad project generates no cash flow, while a good project generates a return ofα$\alpha$, whereα>1$\alpha >1$. Since a good project requires an investment of one and generatesα$\alpha$, I refer toα$\alpha$as the return on a good project. The entrepreneur is not more informed about the project's type than investors. All players have the same prior probability of the project being good,P0$P_{0}$.
To raise funds from each investor, the entrepreneur publicly proposes a contractri,fii=1,2$\left\lbrace \left(r_{i},f_{i}\right)\right\rbrace _{i=1,2}$, whereri$r_{i}$represents the return promised to investori$i$andfi$f_{i}$represents the investment solicited from him. Here,fi$f_{i}$are 0.5 due to investors' capital constraint.9I therefore user1,r2$\left\lbrace r_{1},r_{2}\right\rbrace $to represent the proposed contract. The contract means that if investori$i$accepts his offer and the project is implemented, he needs to invest 0.5. In return, the entrepreneur promises him a payment ofri/2$r_{i}/2$if the project is good and zero if the project is bad. If the project is not implemented, investors keep their money and receive their outside option of zero. Since the entrepreneur needs both investors' acceptance,ri≥1$r_{i}\ge 1$for each investor. Without loss of generality, I assumer1≥r2≥1$r_{1}\ge r_{2}\ge 1$. When the project is good and implemented, the entrepreneur receives the project's residual cash flow,α−r1+r2/2$\alpha -\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right)/2$; otherwise she receives zero. I assume that the entrepreneur does not propose any contract if no contract brings her a positive expected profit in equilibrium. I can therefore focus on contracts withr1+r2<2α$r_{1}+r_{2}<2\alpha$. It follows that whenever a good project is implemented, the cash flow of the project is high enough to pay each investor his promised return.
For the informal illustration here, I focus onP0=0.5$P_{0}=0.5$. First consider flat contracts withr1=r2$r_{1}=r_{2}$. The two investors are completely aligned in the sense that having the same information, they will make the same investment decision. A reasonable equilibrium prediction would be that they always communicate truthfully with each other. The investors then face three scenarios as shown in TableI. If either of them observesG$G$, the posterior probability of a good project is one, if neither of them observes the project's type, which is denoted by(⌀,⌀)$(\varnothing,\varnothing)$, this probability is 0.5, the prior, and if either of them observesB$B$, the probability goes to zero. The investors will not invest when either of them observesB$B$, no matter what the contract is. Accordingly, we can focus on two potential contracts.

In that case, since all good projects are funded, the entrepreneur's expected profit is0.5×α−2$0.5\times \left(\alpha -2\right)$. Note that if2,2$\left\lbrace 2,2\right\rbrace $is offered, the investors earn a positive profit when either of them observesG$G$, which amounts to an information rent since whether an investor observes the project's type is his private information. Nonetheless, ifα$\alpha$is sufficiently high,2,2$\left\lbrace 2,2\right\rbrace $leads to a higher expected profit for the entrepreneur than1,1$\left\lbrace 1,1\right\rbrace $—the greater acceptance rate is more valuable than the forgone information rent.
Can we find a cheaper contract to induce investment at⌀,⌀$\left(\varnothing,\varnothing \right)$than2,2$\left\lbrace 2,2\right\rbrace $? The answer is yes: We can use the hierarchical contract2,2−m$\left\lbrace 2,2-m\right\rbrace $. When a hierarchical contract is offered, the two investors are not necessarily aligned as above. Therefore, rather than assume truthful communication, we need to consider their strategic communication in the cheap-talk game. Note that communication matters only when no investor observesB$B$because the investor who observesB$B$will reject investment right away and the project will not be implemented.
To see that2,2−m$\left\lbrace 2,2-m\right\rbrace $is the cheapest contract to induce investment at⌀,⌀$\left(\varnothing,\varnothing \right)$, note that if2>r1≥r2≥1$2>r_{1}\ge r_{2}\ge 1$, the two investors are aligned in the sense that neither of them would like to invest at⌀,⌀$\left(\varnothing,\varnothing \right)$, so to attain their highest expected profits, they will communicate truthfully and reject their offers at⌀,⌀$\left(\varnothing,\varnothing \right)$. This implies thatr1=2$r_{1}=2$is the minimum to ensure that the investors are not aligned and investor 1 will persuade investor 2 rather than communicate truthfully.
We have obtained two candidates for the optimal contract. Regarding the choice between them, the entrepreneur faces a trade-off between low returns offered to investors and a high probability of financing. Whenα$\alpha$is belowα¯≡1+1−m/2/1−m2$\overline{\alpha }\equiv 1+\left(1-m/2\right)/\left(1-m\right)^{2}$, the opportunity cost of missing an uncertain project is low, so the entrepreneur would prefer to offer investors low returns despite a low probability of financing. The optimal contract is1,1$\left\lbrace 1,1\right\rbrace $in this case. Whenα≥α¯$\alpha \ge \overline{\alpha }$, the entrepreneur would prefer to offer investors high returns to induce a high probability of financing, so the optimal contract is2,2−m$\left\lbrace 2,2-m\right\rbrace $.10
Strategic communication. Note that strategic communication is crucial to the emergence of a hierarchical contract. With the goal to induce investment at(⌀,⌀)$(\varnothing,\varnothing)$, the entrepreneur's optimal contract would be2,2$\left\lbrace 2,2\right\rbrace $if both investors must communicate truthfully, and2−m,2−m$\left\lbrace 2-m,2-m\right\rbrace $if they could not communicate at all.11Here, the entrepreneur benefits from less communication between investors, because communication improves investors' private information and increases their ability to earn information rents. Therefore, if investors can communicate strategically, the entrepreneur would like to prevent communication between them, which is achieved by offering2,2−m$\left\lbrace 2,2-m\right\rbrace $and creating a motive for persuasion. Put intuitively, hierarchies are helpful because they can be used to disincentivize fine communication (that distinguishes observations ofG$G$and⌀$\varnothing$) when investors communicate strategically. When investors communicate exogenously, this motive is absent and hierarchies will not emerge.
Investor 2's informedness. The emergence of a hierarchical contract relies on investor 1's informedness but not on investor 2's. To see the latter, even when investor 2 never observes the project's type,2,2−m$\left\lbrace 2,2-m\right\rbrace $remains the optimal contract to induce investment at(⌀,⌀)$(\varnothing,\varnothing)$and brings the entrepreneur the same expected profit.12In SectionIV, I consider costly information acquisition, which allows some investors to remain uninformed.
Endogenous syndication. The persuasion motive underlying the hierarchical contract also provides a novel motivation for syndication. Suppose that investor 1 has deep pockets, and the entrepreneur solicits the entire investment cost of one from him. The optimal contract isr1=2$r_{1}=2$whenα≥2−m/1−m$\alpha \ge \left(2-m\right)/\left(1-m\right)$andr1=1$r_{1}=1$otherwise.13It is not hard to see that the contractr1=2$r_{1}=2$is strictly dominated by the hierarchical contract2,2−m$\left\lbrace 2,2-m\right\rbrace $. Hence, the entrepreneur may strictly prefer a hierarchical syndicate to a single investor. The motivation for syndication here is to allow for persuasion between investors. According to previous observations, this motivation does not depend on investor 2's informedness and thus is different from the classical motivation that more investors bring in more information. In SectionIV, I also allow for deep-pocketed investors and show that, in some cases, the entrepreneur will optimally resort to a hierarchical syndicate that keeps investor 2 uninformed.
Signal structure. The motivation to form a hierarchical syndicate relies on an investor's observation having at least three possible realizations. The fact that investor 1's communication can partially reveal his observation is key to the motivation. If an investor's observation is binary, his communication either fully reveals his observation or does not reveal it in a pure strategy equilibrium. Thus, when he attempts to persuade the other investor, his communication is completely uninformative and cannot improve investor 2's posterior. In this case, the entrepreneur does not strictly prefer a hierarchical syndicate to a single deep-pocketed investor.
Many investors. The logic of the two-investor case also applies if there are more investors. Suppose that the entrepreneur needs to obtain1/n$1/n$from each ofn$n$investors, wheren≥3$n\ge 3$. If she would like to induce investment only when at least one investor observesG$G$, she proposes the flat contract withri=1$r_{i}=1$. If she would like to induce investment even when no one observes the project's type, she can propose the hierarchical contract withri=1+1−mi−1$r_{i}=1+\left(1-m\right)^{i-1}$. Notably, investor 3's return is lower than that of investor 2. That is because investor 2, who is convinced by investor 1's persuasion, also attempts to persuade investor 3 to invest. Investor 2's persuasion further reduces the return that investor 3 demands. Due to such persuasion cascades, investors' returns are decreasing, and a more delicate hierarchical structure emerges in the optimal contract. In SectionV, I consider the optimal contract in the multiple-investor case.
article.III. The Model Solution of the Baseline:
In this section, I formally solve the entrepreneur's contracting problem in the baseline setup. First, I formulate investors' strategies and specify the solution concept of the game. Second, I solve investors' joint investment game with communication, which corresponds to the part of financing after the entrepreneur proposes a contract. Third, I characterize the entrepreneur's optimal contract.

Definition 1.A contract is called flat if it offers all investors identical returns and hierarchical if it offers investors different returns.

Pareto dominance follows from the notion that investors can coordinate in their best interest, so any sustainable collective improvement should not exist. Such mutually beneficial coordination is naturally expected, given that investors can communicate at no cost. This refinement is also consistent with the theoretical result of Kim and Sobel (1995), which finds that when players with no conflict of interest can have pre-play cheap talk, only the efficient outcome is stable in an evolutionary sense.

The definition of enthusiasm implicitly suggests that enthusiastic investors accept more aggressively than unenthusiastic investors, as the latter do not always accept when no one observesB$B$. This definition requires an examination of all equilibria. The reason is that when both investors are unenthusiastic, one of them may accept when no one observesB$B$in some equilibria because project implementation hinges on the other investor, who accepts only when he knows the project is good. However, an unenthusiastic investor has no preference for accepting so aggressively, so he does not do so in some equilibria. Proposition2characterizes investors' enthusiasm.

Proposition2implies that the number of enthusiastic investors is weakly increasing in investors' promised returns. However, more enthusiastic investors do not necessarily mean a greater likelihood of project implementation. Whenr1≥1/P0>r2$r_{1}\ge 1/P_{0}>r_{2}$, investor 1 becomes enthusiastic and would like to persuade investor 2 to accept. The persuasion has ambiguous effects on project implementation. On the one hand, it lowers the return demanded by investor 2 to be enthusiastic from1/P0$1/P_{0}$to1+1−P0P01−m1$1+\frac{1-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\left(1-m_{1}\right)$. On the other hand, ifr2<1+1−P0P01−m1$r_{2}<1+\frac{1-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\left(1-m_{1}\right)$and investor 2 is not convinced to be enthusiastic, he will ignore investor 1's communication and accept only when he observesG$G$. That means that the project is implemented only when investor 2 observesG$G$. In contrast, when neither investor is enthusiastic, the project is implemented when either investor observesG$G$. Making only one investor enthusiastic renders project implementation even less likely than making no investor enthusiastic. However, making both investors enthusiastic does increase the likelihood.
In investors' joint investment game with communication, there are two layers of information revelation. The first layer involves investors' acceptance decisions. An investor who observesB$B$must reject his offer, in which case the project is not implemented. Hence, conditional on project implementation, no investor observesB$B$, which bolsters other investor's confidence in the project. This layer of information revelation is similar in spirit to the core mechanisms of Caillaud and Tirole (2007) and Kremer, Mansour, and Perry (2014). The second layer involves unverifiable communication. Unverifiable communication is subject to conflicts of interest between investors. When investors' interests are aligned, they can coordinate and ensure the sharing of all useful information. When investors have divergent interests, the investor with the better contract terms would like to persuade the other investor to accept in some cases. In this case, this investor's communication does not reveal more information than what his action reveals.
In addition to leading to a unique investment outcome, all of the possible equilibria also satisfy no regret ex post. That is, knowing other investors' acceptance decisions ex post, any investor would not like to change his communication strategy or acceptance decision. This implies that the investment outcome will not change even if investors can communicate multiple times or move sequentially. This property is especially important for applications like private financing, where acceptance decisions are observable to others, investment promises are not perfectly binding or enforceable, and the order of investors' moves is not prespecified.16
For the entrepreneur's contracting problem, only the investment outcomes induced by contracts matter. According to Proposition1and Proposition2, the investment outcomes as well as investors' enthusiasm are uniquely determined by the contract. Depending on what fraction of investors are enthusiastic in equilibrium, I define three types of contracts as follows.

Note thatP01−P0(α−1)$\frac{P_{0}}{1-P_{0}}(\alpha -1)$is increasing in both the return of a good project,α$\alpha$, and the prior probability of a good project,P0$P_{0}$. Intuitively, it captures the notion of a project's ex ante quality. When the project has low ex ante quality, the opportunity cost of missing an uncertain project is low. Therefore, the entrepreneur chooses to promise low returns to investors despite stringent screening. Investors have high thresholds for investing, that is, are both unenthusiastic in our model language. In contrast, when the project has high ex ante quality, the opportunity cost of missing an uncertain project is high. Therefore, the entrepreneur chooses to promise high returns to investors for lax screening. Investors have low thresholds for investing, that is, are both enthusiastic in our model language.
In the former case, investors have high thresholds for investing and there is little room for information rents. Out of the concern for the high thresholds, the entrepreneur hopes that one investor's observation of a good project can be credibly communicated to the other investor. To facilitate truthful communication, the entrepreneur would like to homogenize investors and align their interests. As implied by Proposition3, the optimal contract must be flat, under which investors are offered identical returns and communicate truthfully.
In the latter case, investors have low thresholds for investing and there is substantial room for information rents because investors' observations are possibly better than their thresholds. The root of the rents is investors' private information, which depends on not only their own observation but also others' communication. In this scenario, truthful communication improves the precision of investors' information and thus enhances their ability to extract information rents. To impede truthful communication, the entrepreneur would like to differentiate investors and create conflicts of interest. As implied by Proposition3, the optimal contract must be hierarchical, under which the investor with the higher promised return attempts to persuade the other investor to accept and pools the observation of a good project with no observation.
article.IV. Endogenous Syndication:
In the baseline setup, I assume that investors are subject to a capital constraint, so the entrepreneur must raise funds from a syndicate with two investors to implement the project. Put differently, syndication is exogenously required. In this section, I relax the capital constraint and show that syndication is endogenously chosen by the entrepreneur in many cases. This analysis illustrates a new motivation for syndication—allowing for persuasion between investors.
The setup with endogenous syndication differs from the baseline setup in two respects. First, both investors are deep-pocketed, so the entrepreneur can choose to approach only one investor or two investors, and in her offer to each investori$i$,fi$f_{i}$can be any nonnegative finite value. Without loss of generality, I require the total solicited investment to be at least one. Still, a bad project generates no cash flow while a good project generatesα$\alpha$, whereα>1$\alpha >1$. That means investing one in a good project generates a fixed surplusα−1$\alpha -1$, but any investment on top of 1 yields negative additional surplus.
Second, each investor's information is acquired through a costly action rather than being exogenously given. Specifically, if the entrepreneur provides investori$i$with information access to the project, investori$i$can choose to acquire information. He observes the project's type with probabilitymi=m∈0,1$m_{i}=m\in \left(0,1\right)$by paying a fixed costc$c$, and with probabilitymi=0$m_{i}=0$otherwise. I assume thatc<(1−P0)m$c<(1-P_{0})m$, which ensures that the cost is not so high that information acquisition can be induced in equilibrium. If the entrepreneur does not provide investori$i$with information access,mi=0$m_{i}=0$. Obviously, investori$i$withmi=0$m_{i}=0$always observesOi=⌀$O_{i}=\varnothing$. Probabilitymi$m_{i}$is neither observable nor verifiable to others. I say an investor's information acquisition is successful if he observes the project's type. As in the baseline setup, successful information acquisition is independent across investors. ThenPM≜1−1−mM$P\left(M\right)\triangleq 1-\left(1-m\right)^{M}$represents the probability that at least one ofM∈0,1,2$M\in \left\lbrace 0,1,2\right\rbrace $investors withmi=m$m_{i}=m$observes the project's type. Further, letΔP(M)≡PM−PM−1$\Delta P(M)\equiv P\left(M\right)-P\left(M-1\right)$be the difference made by one additional investor with information.
Depending on the number of investors solicited by the entrepreneur, I define “single-investor contracts” and “syndicate contracts” as follows.
Definition 5.A contract is a single-investor contract if only one investor is solicited by the entrepreneur; otherwise, it is a syndicate contract.

To obtain the entrepreneur's strict preference for syndication, I stipulate that the entrepreneur proposes a syndicate contract only when it is optimal and strictly dominates any single-investor contract. Notice that if a syndicate contract has an investor who is solicited for zero investment or never invests in equilibrium, this investor does not acquire information or contribute funds. That means that this syndicate contract brings the entrepreneur the same expected profit as a single-investor contract. I can therefore focus on single-investor contracts and syndicate contracts under which both investors are solicited for positive investments and may invest in equilibrium. Without loss of generality, I require that allri$r_{i}$in the contract be at least one; otherwise, investori$i$will never invest.
Investors who do not receive the recommendation to acquire information are not provided with information access and thus cannot acquire information. For those who do receive the recommendation, the contract proposed by the entrepreneur needs to ensure that they would like to acquire information. Since information acquisition is neither observable nor verifiable to others, an investor can always deviate and keep the other investor uninformed of his deviation. I refer to this type of deviation as private deviation.
The model is solved by backward induction as follows. First, for any contract, I solve investors' joint investment game with communication, assuming that the investors follow the entrepreneur's recommendationmii=1,2$\left\lbrace m_{i}\right\rbrace {}_{i=1,2}$. Second, I derive necessary and sufficient conditions for a contract to prevent private deviation. Third, I derive the optimal contract, which maximizes the entrepreneur's expected payoff among all contracts preventing private deviation. I show that under the optimal contract, any equilibrium in which some investors deviate from recommendations cannot survive Pareto dominance. Therefore, the optimal contract always induces a unique investment outcome.
For single-investor contracts and syndicate contracts under which the two investors are aligned (1/P0>r1≥r2$1/P_{0}>r_{1}\ge r_{2}$, orr1≥r2≥1/P0$r_{1}\ge r_{2}\ge 1/P_{0}$), the equilibrium investment outcome is straightforward. When the two investors are not aligned (r1≥1/P0>r2$r_{1}\ge 1/P_{0}>r_{2}$), ifmaxf1,f2<1$\max \left\lbrace f_{1},f_{2}\right\rbrace <1$, project implementation requires both investors' acceptance and the equilibrium characterization is consistent with Propositions1and2, while ifmaxf1,f2≥1$\max \left\lbrace f_{1},f_{2}\right\rbrace \ge 1$, one investor—say investori$i$—can implement the project alone. This difference changes the game in two respects. First, investori$i$has no incentive to persuade the other to accept or not in any case, since whenever he accepts, the other investor's acceptance decision does not affect project implementation. Second, investori$i$cannot infer any information from the project implementation when he accepts.

Next, I derive necessary and sufficient conditions for a contract to prevent private deviation. Suppose that an investor does privately deviate from his recommendation. On the one hand, since the other investor cannot observe whether the deviating investor actually acquires information, the other investor will still have the same belief about his information acquisition, communication, and acceptance strategies. Hence, the other investor will still follow the recommendation and his equilibrium communication and acceptance strategies. On the other hand, the deviating investor knows that the other investor will still follow the recommendation and the equilibrium communication and acceptance strategies. The only difference is that he always observes⌀$\varnothing$. Within the class of all-enthusiast contracts, the motivation for syndication is to allow for persuasion between investors. For the entrepreneur, investors' information is double-edged. The benefit of the information is to help investors avoid investing in a bad project and thus make them more eager to invest. The cost of the information is to enhance investors' ability to earn information rents. Persuasion between investors in a syndicate reinforces the benefit more than the cost. To see this, in the model investor 1's rejection upon observingB$B$helps investor 2 avoid investing in some bad projects, and his pooling ofG$G$and⌀$\varnothing$prevents investor 2 from earning information rents.19That is why a syndicate with persuasion between investors is more desirable for the entrepreneur than a single investor. Notably, this motivation for syndication is distinct from the classical motivations such as capital constraints (investor 1 has deep pockets here) and “second opinion” (investor 2 has no information here), and it justifies the existence of syndication and pure capital providers without resorting to capital constraints or risk aversion.
article.V. Multiple Investors:
This section extends the baseline setup with multiple investors. When there are more than two investors, figuring out how investors communicate becomes much more challenging, as the communication between any pair of investors is also affected by the communication from others. I find that investors' persuasion motive gives rise to persuasion cascades in equilibrium: An investor who becomes enthusiastic due to another enthusiastic investor's persuasion will attempt to persuade others to accept and possibly make more investors enthusiastic. The entrepreneur's optimal contracting based on persuasion cascades mostly follows the intuition presented in SectionIIIbut differentiates investors in finer ways.

article.VI. Discussion:
In this section, I discuss how the model relates to empirical observations and I derive testable implications. The discussion is based on the baseline setup in SectionII.
Syndicated loans and VC syndicates are two common forms of syndicates. Roughly 78% of loans in the universe of DealScan and 65% of VC deals in the PitchBook database were syndicated. Large and mature firms are the typical borrowers of syndicated loans, which feature low returns and low risk. In contrast, early-stage startups are the typical targets of VC investments, which feature high returns upon success and low probabilities of success.
A direct application of the model requires knowledge of projects' ex ante quality. Unfortunately, this is not generally available because we do not observe projects that are not funded. The following proposition implies that the syndicate structure is associated with a project's acceptance probability in equilibrium.
Proposition 9.Givenm$m$andα$\alpha$(P0)$P_{0})$, asP0$P_{0}$(α$\alpha$) increases, the project's acceptance probability weakly increases, and the optimal syndicate structure switches from a flat structure to a hierarchical structure.

The literature sheds some light on projects' acceptance probability. According to Berg (2018), in a large German bank, the acceptance probability for the sample of loans worth more than EUR 1 million is close to 80%. In contrast, according to Gompers et al. (2020), for each deal closed by VC investors, 101 were considered, of which 28 moved forward to a management meeting, 10 proceeded to a review with VC partners, and five advanced to due diligence, indicating a much lower acceptance probability than for big loans. With this information, the model predicts that a typical syndicated loan should have a hierarchical structure and a typical VC syndicate should have a flat structure. These predictions are consistent with empirical observations. The vast majority of VC syndicates are organized inflat structures: Investors in the same round almost always invest at the same firm valuation. In contrast, most loan syndicates are organized inhierarchical structuresin which lead investors earn higher returns than others through fee income.22
Here it is helpful to contrast my explanation of syndicate structures with other potential explanations. A typical view on syndicated loans is that lead banks receive fees as compensation for their effort to organize syndicates, negotiate terms, screen deals, and so on. In essence, this compensation-based explanation implies that the investor that takes more responsibility in a syndicate should be compensated more. However, this implication is at odds with the typical return structure of VC syndicates in which all investors receive identical returns. Therefore, although the compensation-based explanation makes much sense and is likely important in some situations, it is insufficient for understanding different observed syndication practices. My communication-based theory uncovers another role of a syndicate's return structure: affecting investors' incentives to share information. It relates a syndicate's return structure to the entrepreneur's preferred communication pattern and further to the project's ex ante quality.
Apart from the pattern of syndicate structures, the logic of hierarchical structures in the model also squares with a salient feature of syndicated loans—underwriting. One of the primary functions of underwriters is to organize syndicates and help convince other investors to invest. By appointing underwriters, the entrepreneur provides high underwriting fees to financially align the underwriters with her and deliberately obligates lead investors to act in her interest. Therefore, my theory can also be interpreted as a theory for underwriting that addresses why and under which scenarios underwriting arises.
The model predicts that underwriting may result in communication problems between underwriters and other lenders. In practice, practitioners and legal scholars have long been concerned with communication problems in syndicated loans. A famous example is IFE Fund v. Goldman Sachs International (Petkovic (2008)).23In 2000, Goldman arranged a syndicate to enable an acquisition in Europe, and IFE participated as a lender. The auditor reported to Goldman that due diligence was progressing slowly due to a lack of access to the management team. The reports resembled an observation of⌀$\varnothing$for Goldman because it implied that the deal's quality was uncertain. Receiving considerable arrangement fees, Goldman still wanted to close the deal despite the uncertainty. However, Goldman worried that the reports may have been too gloomy for other lenders, as someone internally noted that they are “not going to help syndication.” Goldman withheld the reports from other lenders, which resembles communicatingG$G$instead of⌀$\varnothing$in the model. Without hearing bad news, other lenders chose to proceed. The deal was closed as planned but turned out to be a financial disaster ex post.24
A key prediction of the model is that investors in a syndicate may receive different returns, and the return difference is related to investors' strategic communication of unverifiable information. As discussed before, the return difference usually takes the form of fees (e.g., in syndicated loans). In this subsection, I derive testable implications for the return difference. In the baseline setup, the return difference when the optimal contract is hierarchical ism1−P0/P0$m\left(1-P_{0}\right)/P_{0}$, from which I readily obtain the following implication.
Proposition 10.The return difference decreases in the prior probability of a good project and increases in the probability that the lead investor observes the project's type.

To obtain more implications, I extend the model along two dimensions. First, I introduce a cost of strategic communication. The cost of strategic communication may take several possible forms in practice. It could be a loss in reputation if an investor's claim turns out to be false ex post, or it could be a legal punishment because arrangers may be held accountable for misrepresentation in some jurisdictions. Suppose that an investor suffers a loss ofη$\eta$if he communicates that he observesG$G$upon observing nothing. The following proposition states that the cost of strategic communication increases the return difference in hierarchical contracts and also makes hierarchical contracts less attractive for the entrepreneur.

article.VII. Conclusion and Other Applications:
The core of my theory is a model with a single principal, multiple agents, and externalities between agents. The agents, who are investors in the model, own private information and communicate with each other strategically. Since an agent cares about others' decisions, he may not truthfully communicate unverifiable information to others if they have conflicts of interest. As pointed out by the vast literature on information design (Rayo and Segal (2010), Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011)), controlling agents' information flow could help the principal influence their decisions. In many cases, the principal cannot control side communication between agents. However, she can potentially use contracts to determine whether agents have aligned or divergent interests and thereby affect how agents communicate strategically. This idea underlies my theory of multiagent contracting.
Although my theory is formulated in the context of an entrepreneur raising capital from multiple investors, it can be broadly applied to other settings. For example, in organizational economics, a firm owner contracts with multiple employees who work on a joint task. Employees' payoffs depend on the result of the task (e.g., through bonus or promotion). Employees can evaluate the likelihood of success based on their knowledge and share it with others by strategic communication. How should the firm owner design an organizational structure to motivate employees' efforts? My theory suggests that for firms whose projects have low ex ante quality, the firm owner should keep the firm organization flat and facilitate communication between employees. In contrast, for firms whose projects have high ex ante quality, the firm owner should build a hierarchy inside the firm by appointing multiple levels of management. Managers can earn more from the success of the task and thus help the firm owner persuade other employees to exert effort. My theory provides a new perspective on why and how firm organizations evolve.

article.Proof of Proposition6: Finally, I show that under the optimal contract, any equilibrium in which some investors deviate from recommendations cannot survive Pareto dominance. This is trivially true if the optimal contract is a single-investor contract. Suppose that the optimal contract is the all-enthusiast syndicate contract in Proposition6, which recommends only investor 1 to acquire information. Consider any equilibrium in which investor 1 does not acquire information. Then investor 2 will never invest, sinceP0r2<1$P_{0}r_{2}<1$and he expects the project to be good with probabilityP0$P_{0}$whenever the project is implemented. Hence, the project will never be implemented, and both investors receive their outside option, zero. Due to the tie-breaking rule, both investors prefer investor 1 to acquire information. That means that, any such equilibrium is Pareto dominated by the one in which investor 1 acquires information.

article.A. Syndicated Loans:
In a syndicated loan, two or more banks jointly extend credit to a borrower. Although all lenders share a single set of legal documents, their obligations are several; individual banks are not responsible for other banks' obligations. The syndication process can take from one to nine months. First, a prospective borrower selects a lead arranger to advise and manage the syndication process. In most cases, this mandate is awarded based on competitive bidding among the borrower' s principal relationship banks or other banks with relevant expertise. At times, borrowers request that more than one bank share the lead mandate (also known as joint mandates) to maximize the chance of a successful syndication or to reward particular banks with lead status and higher compensation. The lead arranger is responsible for negotiating key terms and covenants with the borrower, in addition to analyzing credit quality, but each bank participating in the syndicate is ultimately responsible for its own credit analysis and review of the documentation.
A salient feature of syndicated loans is that borrowers usually pay various fees to lenders in addition to loan interest. Some fees (participation fees and facility fees) are allocated with respect to the amount that a lender finally contributes, while others (arrangement fees and underwriting fees) are apportioned with respect to a lender's position or function in the syndicate. According to Berg, Saunders, and Steffen (2016), the average upfront fee reported by DealScan is 61 bps, while the average interest rate spread is 190 bps (on top of LIBOR). The allocation of fees usually involves significant inequalities in many deals. McDonald (1982) demonstrates that in a typical syndicated loan, the arrangers retain over 76% of fees while loaning 52% of the funds. Fight (2004) illustrates that the arrangers earn nearly double the yield in fees compared with those that merely lend. Esty (2001) illustrates how fees are allocated in loan syndicates, taking the Hong Kong Disneyland project loan as an example. Because the allocation of fees was not disclosed to protect confidentiality, the fee structure in this case study is based on the author's estimate assuming an upfront fee of 125 bps. TableCIpresents the allocation of commitment and fees in this loan syndicate. According to their roles in the syndicate, investors can be roughly divided into three tiers. The top tier included only the mandated arranger, Chase. Chase had a relatively large final hold position and received a large number of fees, which amounted to 934 bps of its final hold position. Another six big banks in the second tier had the same final hold positions as Chase but received much lower fees, which amounted to 169 bps. The other 25 banks in the third tier had smaller final hold positions and received low fees, only 50 to 70 bps. All these numbers together imply that in addition to prestigious titles, arrangers earn substantially higher returns than other lenders in loan syndicates.
article.B. VC Syndicates: Syndication is standard practice in entrepreneurial finance, especially VC investing. VC syndicates are similar to loan syndicates in many aspects. For each round of financing, two or more investors jointly invest in a firm under a single set of legal documents, but their obligations are several. Usually, one VC serves as the lead investor, who takes a large fraction of the responsibility for negotiating valuation and terms, due diligence, legal work, and other formalities of the investment. In a syndicate, the lead investor is usually the most reputable or the most aggressive one, and it contributes the largest amount of capital. Unlike fees in loan syndicates, no fee is paid to investors in most cases, and thus investors in the same round obtain the same cash flow right as well as voting power per unit of capital invested. Due to the indivisibility of board seats, the lead investor usually takes the board seat assigned to his round as the representative of the whole syndicate.
